Title: Stoner
Author: John  Williams
My Review:
Now that was different… if you’re looking for a book that will blow your mind or endlessly entertain you, this is not the one.

The writing was undeniably beautiful, but in a quiet and non-flashy way. Just a man living an unremarkable life that could be viewed as both peaceful and depressing.

I have never finished a book with so little resolution, yet so much to think about. I can’t even say that I liked it, but not once did I consider putting it down.

As with most thrillers, The Firm was an easy and enjoyable read but not much to write home about.

My previous experiences with the genre have typically been more cat-and-mouse detective books, the crime-conglomerate and law focus did provide some welcomed novelty.

Title: The Fall of Hyperion (Hyperion Cantos, #2)
Author: Dan Simmons
My Review:
A lot of what I liked about the first novel also held true (to an extent) in The Fall of Hyperion: the writing, the character depth and the overall twisted nature of The Shrike.

HOWEVER… There was simply too much going on in this one. I didn’t really care as much about the fate of the universe, politics, etc. I was more invested in the pilgrims and Shrike and I wish Simmons had focused solely on that angle.

It felt a bit overcomplicated but overall this really was an impressive and ambitious first half (books 1 & 2) of the series. Will consider books 3 & 4 at a later stage.

Title: Hyperion (Hyperion Cantos, #1)
Author: Dan Simmons
My Review:
An extremely ambitious and obscure novel. Did I love it? Yes. Am I confused? Absolutely. Is it for everybody? No.

I really enjoyed the structure, where each character had their own tale somehow relating to the overarching story. This Canterbury-style framework gives a literary weight to each character’s history, as if the novel were a collection of timeless classics woven together.

My favourite of the tales were:
- The Priest's Tale: The Man Who Cried God
- The Scholar's Tale: The River Lethe's Taste is Bitter

Some of the ideas and sub-plots explored in this novel were absolutely harrowing, I loved that and it was a pleasant throwback to my Stephen King reading stint. Speaking of which, I definitely got Dark Tower vibes from this book, not in plot but in atmosphere. It has that same sense of niche cosmic weirdness that somehow just works.

Author: Edwin Lefèvre

Title: A Promised Land
Author: Barack Obama

Title: The Stand
Author: Stephen  King
My Review:
An absolute masterpiece and my favourite Stephen King book.

That grand-scale carnage and devastation scratched an itch I never even knew existed. Those early interlude chapters that zoom out from the main cast and show the superflu wiping out everyday people were so twisted...

I didn't love the supernatural elements and think King could've just stuck with the superflu, but it didn't detract from too much from this unbelievable reading experience.

Author: Phil Knight

Title: American Prometheus: The Triumph and Tragedy of J. Robert Oppenheimer
Author: Kai Bird
My Review:
The man who changed the nature of warfare and politics forever...

This was a great read, but 700+ pages (especially a non-fiction) is too long for my liking.

Title: Steve Jobs
Author: Walter Isaacson
My Review:
Once again Walter Isaacson has done an exceptional job at dissecting a very complicated man.

Steve Jobs was often not the most likeable man, but he undoubtedly changed the world forever. His story was an absolute pleasure to read as a tech enthusiast and long-time Apple user.

Title: Red Notice: A True Story of High Finance, Murder, and One Man's Fight for Justice
Author: Bill Browder
My Review:
This is about as wild as a biography gets, so much so that it sometimes read like a fiction novel!

It was eye-opening learning about the level of power held by the Russian government, I also enjoyed the hedge fund aspect in the beginning.

Title: Wizard and Glass (The Dark Tower, #4)
Author: Stephen  King
My Review:
Hands down my favourite book of the series (so far). I was hesitant to get into this because I knew it focused on Roland's past rather than a continuation of the story, but I am so glad that I finally read it!

This story was so dark, so twisted and just as heart breaking.

Rhea of the Cöos is easily one of the most unsettling characters in the entire series, and that’s saying a lot for Stephen King...

Title: A Dance with Dragons (A Song of Ice and Fire, #5)
Author: George R.R. Martin
My Review:
WHOLE SERIES REVIEW (BOOKS 1-5)

A masterpiece but not an easy read!

George R.R. Martin wrote some brilliant characters and a tale of epic proportion. It’s dark, brutal, unpredictable, and filled with such complex individuals. Experiencing the story through unique character viewpoints was a deep experience.

The only real downside is the length. Each book is massive, and the decision to split the last two by character (instead of timeline) made it feel painfully slow at times. But even then, it never stopped being impressive.

Title: Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?
Author: Philip K. Dick
My Review:
Not your typical sci-fi novel!

I liked the philosophical aspect of the book and the general dystopian feel. However, the actual story and characters themselves lacked a certain depth.

Also, what a cool book title.

Title: Trading and Exchanges: Market Microstructure for Practitioners
Author: Larry Harris
My Review:
This was a tough read but will serve as an extremely good foundation for anything market related.

The deep dive into liquidity was fascinating, and learning about market makers really stuck with me.

Title: The Martian
Author: Andy Weir
My Review:
A fun and well executed story.

I found it quite predictable and was never truly feared for Watney. But his problem solving was entertaining.

Title: Educated
Author: Tara Westover
My Review:
One of the best biographies out there, and a very easy non-fiction read.

It was interesting getting a peak into the world of extreme Mormon survivalism (which to me is just so wild), but also an all-round touching and inspiring story.

Title: The Return of the King (The Lord of the Rings, #3)
Author: J.R.R. Tolkien
My Review:
WHOLE SERIES REVIEW

Let me start off by saying that I wish I had not watched the movies prior to reading this series. Knowing how things end detracted from the suspense and made it feel like I was observing the story, rather than living it. I particularly felt this in the final novel, where the ending (by definition) plays a larger role.

That being said, the series is still a masterpiece. The world building is breathtaking, but what really had a profound impact on me were the hobbits themselves. Their sincerity, simplicity and refusal to complicate life gave me a childlike sense of wonder.

This lighthearted nature is not something I typically strive for in my reading, but Tolkien's ability to juxtapose innocence with evil was executed perfectly.

The Ring, too, struck me on a deeper level. Its power functions almost like a drug: seductive at first, then isolating, then destructive. Watching beloved characters slowly lose themselves to its influence was a unique portrayal of addiction, one that felt surprisingly modern.

Title: Crime and Punishment
Author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
My Review:
A gripping but brutal portrayal of a man losing his mind and trying to find redemption.

The writing was extremely good and I was very invested in Raskolnikov's character, but seeing the someone's psyche deteriorate to such lows is no easy task. It was a great book, but not one that I enjoyed reading (not in the typical sense at least)

The Russian names confused me very much, I couldn't seem to make associations like I would with an English book.

Title: Twelve Angry Men
Author: Reginald Rose
My Review:
An excellent dissection of American legal system and society.

This book left me with so many questions: Does the American justice system truly work? Can anyone ever be completely objective? And what would have happened if there hadn’t been an 8th juror? I think that was the whole point.

Books written in the form of play scripts are certainly not my favourite but I did not mind making an exception here. The fact that it is such a quick read also helped make the format more tolerable.

Title: 3001: The Final Odyssey
Author: Arthur C. Clarke
My Review:
WHOLE SERIES REVIEW

This was my first ever space opera style sci-fi series and it did not disappoint! Very big ideas were explored and there's something very special reading about mankind landing on one of Jupiter's moons.

The scale of the story often had me in awe and I particularly liked the portrayal of AI, very impressive for a book published in the 60s. However, the lack of character depth and inconsistent pacing were at times off-putting.

Definitely worth a read!

Title: The Bitcoin Standard: The Decentralized Alternative to Central Banking
Author: Saifedean Ammous
My Review: An interesting read and a great deep dive into the fundamentals of currency, something we take for granted in today's age. Taking a step back and asking "is this the best we can do?" was a worthwhile exercise.

Title: Death's End (Remembrance of Earth’s Past, #3)
Author: Liu Cixin
My Review:
WHOLE SERIES REVIEW

I recently learned the term "hard sci-fi": A subgenre of science fiction writing that emphasizes scientific accuracy and precise technical detail.

That is EXACTLY what this series is, the hardest sci-fi you may ever find. So hard that it made me hard... (intellectually). All ~1500 pages were a mentally stimulating masterpiece, while still maintaining a poetic feel at times.

I enjoyed all 3 books, but book #2 (The Dark Forest) really stood out for me. Concepts like "Wallfacers" and the "Dark Forrest Theory" remain two of the coolest ideas I’ve ever encountered in fiction.

I couldn't believe learning that what I was reading was English translated from Mandarin... It read so smoothly that it felt like the original. Gave me a very interesting perspective on language and Chinese culture.

I couldn't have a higher degree of respect for an author and a hard sci-fi series.

Title: Dune (Dune, #1)
Author: Frank Herbert
My Review:
A truly special flavour of sci-fi with an incredible level of breadth.

Herbert manages to bring together politics, religion, destiny, ecology, and economics in a surprisingly easy-to-follow space opera. What’s more, the novel is done in such a way that it can serve as either a stand-alone story or an intro into (what I can imagine is) a much deeper universe and storyline.

I felt a genuine attachment to the characters, something that’s often lost in big-idea sci-fi. The balance between scientific concept and the human condition is handled very well.

Conceptually, I loved the spice, the sandworms, and the feudal power structures.

If I had to sum up the novel in one sentence: A beautiful threesome between Game of Thrones, Arthur C. Clarke and the Bible.
